html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Twenty-Five — Part One

The Book of Iyov

IYOV

Wisdom — The Covenant of Suffering

Chapters 1 through 14

YHWH Gave and YHWH Has Taken Away — Blessed Be the Name of YHWH — Does Iyov Fear Creator for No Reason — Though He Slay Me I Will Hope in Him — If a Man Dies Will He Live Again

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls — 4QpaleoJobc — 2QJob — 11QtgJob Aramaic Targum

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon

Masoretic Text

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

IYOV — THE PROLOGUE AND THE FIRST CYCLE — CHAPTERS 1 THROUGH 14

IYOV — CHAPTER 1 There Was a Man in the Land of Utz — His Name Was Iyov — The Sons of Creator Came to Present Themselves — The Adversary Also Came — Have You Considered My Servant Iyov — YHWH Has Taken Away — Blessed Be the Name of YHWH

1 There was a man in the land of Utz whose name was Iyov. And that man was blameless and upright, one who feared Creator and turned away from evil.

2 There were born to him seven sons and three daughters.

3 He possessed 7,000 sheep, 3,000 camels, 500 yoke of oxen, and 500 female donkeys, and very many servants, so that this man was the greatest of all the people of the east.

4 His sons used to go and hold a feast in the house of each one on his day, and they would send and invite their three sisters to eat and drink with them.

5 And when the days of the feast had run their course, Iyov would send and consecrate them, and he would rise early in the morning and offer burnt offerings according to the number of them all. For Iyov said, It may be that my sons have sinned, and cursed Creator in their hearts. Thus Iyov did continually.

6 Now there was a day when the sons of Creator came to present themselves before YHWH, and the Adversary also came among them.

7 YHWH said to the Adversary, From where have you come? The Adversary answered YHWH and said, From going to and fro on the earth, and from walking up and down on it.

8 And YHWH said to the Adversary, Have you considered my servant Iyov, that there is none like him on the earth, a blameless and upright man, who fears Creator and turns away from evil?

9 Then the Adversary answered YHWH and said, Does Iyov fear Creator for no reason?

10 Have you not put a hedge around him and his house and all that he has, on every side? You have blessed the work of his hands, and his possessions have increased in the land.

11 But stretch out your hand and touch all that he has, and he will curse you to your face.

12 And YHWH said to the Adversary, Behold, all that he has is in your hand. Only against him do not stretch out your hand. So the Adversary went out from the presence of YHWH.

13 Now there was a day when his sons and daughters were eating and drinking wine in their oldest brother's house,

14 And there came a messenger to Iyov and said, The oxen were plowing and the donkeys feeding beside them,

15 And the Sabeans fell upon them and took them and struck down the servants with the edge of the sword, and I alone have escaped to tell you.

16 While he was yet speaking, there came another and said, The fire of Creator fell from heaven and burned up the sheep and the servants and consumed them, and I alone have escaped to tell you.

17 While he was yet speaking, there came another and said, The Chaldeans formed three groups and made a raid on the camels and took them and struck down the servants with the edge of the sword, and I alone have escaped to tell you.

18 While he was yet speaking, there came another and said, Your sons and daughters were eating and drinking wine in their oldest brother's house,

19 And behold, a great wind came across the wilderness and struck the four corners of the house, and it fell upon the young people, and they are dead, and I alone have escaped to tell you.

20 Then Iyov arose and tore his robe and shaved his head and fell on the ground and worshiped.

21 And he said, Naked I came from my mother's womb, and naked shall I return. YHWH gave, and YHWH has taken away. Blessed be the name of YHWH.

22 In all this Iyov did not sin or charge Creator with wrong.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Dead Sea Scrolls preserve fragments of Iyov from Cave 4 — 4QpaleoJobc and 2QJob — confirming the canonical text. An Aramaic Targum of Iyov was also found at Qumran — 11QtgJob — the oldest known Targum of any biblical book. The Iyov text is among the most ancient covenant literary traditions: the land of Utz and the covenant character of Iyov reflect a pre-Mosaic covenant setting within the Afro-Asiatic covenant corridor.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Iyov Chapter 1 in full. Verse 21 — YHWH gave, and YHWH has taken away. Blessed be the name of YHWH. The covenant response of Iyov to total covenant loss is the theological baseline against which the entire book measures his subsequent covenant struggle. The covenant blessing of YHWH's name at the covenant moment of maximum covenant loss is the covenant standard the entire book will test.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 1 in full. Verse 8 — have you considered my servant Iyov, that there is none like him on the earth, a blameless and upright man, who fears Creator and turns away from evil? YHWH's own testimony about Iyov: the suffering of Iyov is not the consequence of sin or covenant failure. YHWH himself establishes Iyov's covenant integrity before the covenant test begins.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — does Iyov fear Creator for no reason? The Adversary's question is the theological question the entire book answers: is covenant faithful worship of YHWH possible without covenant reward? The Adversary's accusation is that all covenant religion is ultimately transactional — people serve YHWH only because YHWH blesses them. The entire book of Iyov is the covenant refutation of this accusation through the covenant life of one covenant man.

IYOV — CHAPTER 2 The Adversary Came Again — Stretch Out Your Hand and Touch His Bone and His Flesh — YHWH Said Behold He Is in Your Hand — Iyov Was Struck With Loathsome Sores — His Wife Said Curse Creator and Die — Three Friends Came to Console Him

1 Again there was a day when the sons of Creator came to present themselves before YHWH, and the Adversary also came among them to present himself before YHWH.

2 And YHWH said to the Adversary, From where have you come? The Adversary answered YHWH and said, From going to and fro on the earth, and from walking up and down on it.

3 And YHWH said to the Adversary, Have you considered my servant Iyov, that there is none like him on the earth, a blameless and upright man, who fears Creator and turns away from evil? He still holds fast his integrity, although you incited me against him to destroy him without reason.

4 Then the Adversary answered YHWH and said, Skin for skin. All that a man has he will give for his life.

5 But stretch out your hand and touch his bone and his flesh, and he will curse you to your face.

6 And YHWH said to the Adversary, Behold, he is in your hand. Only spare his life.

7 So the Adversary went out from the presence of YHWH and struck Iyov with loathsome sores from the sole of his foot to the crown of his head.

8 And he took a piece of broken pottery with which to scrape himself while he sat in the ashes.

9 Then his wife said to him, Do you still hold fast your integrity? Curse Creator and die.

10 But he said to her, You speak as one of the foolish women would speak. Shall we receive good from Creator, and shall we not receive evil? In all this Iyov did not sin with his lips.

11 Now when Iyov's three friends heard of all this evil that had come upon him, they came each one from his own place, Elifaz the Temanite, Bildad the Shuhite, and Tzofar the Na'amathite. They made an appointment together to come to show him sympathy and comfort him.

12 And when they saw him from a distance, they did not recognize him. And they raised their voices and wept, and they tore their robes and sprinkled dust on their heads toward heaven.

13 And they sat with him on the ground seven days and seven nights, and no one spoke a word to him, for they saw that his suffering was very great.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Aramaic Targum of Iyov from Qumran — 11QtgJob — confirms the canonical text of Chapter 2. The covenant prologue of Chapters 1-2 frames the entire covenant poetic dialogue that follows: the covenant reader knows what Iyov does not know — that his covenant suffering is not covenant punishment but covenant testing. The covenant dramatic irony of the entire book depends on this covenant prologue's covenant privileged information.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 2 in full. Verse 10 — shall we receive good from Creator, and shall we not receive evil? Iyov's covenant second response is the covenant complement of the first: the first response to covenant external loss is covenant blessing YHWH — the second response to covenant physical suffering is covenant acceptance of Creator's sovereign covenant administration of both covenant good and covenant evil.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 2 in full. Verse 13 — they sat with him on the ground seven days and seven nights, and no one spoke a word to him, for they saw that his suffering was very great. The covenant seven-day covenant silence of the three friends is the covenant wisest thing they do in the entire book. The covenant friends who are silent with a suffering person demonstrate more covenant wisdom than the friends who speak. Their covenant theology becomes dangerous when they open their mouths.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3 — you incited me against him to destroy him without reason. YHWH's own admission that the Adversary incited him to test Iyov without reason — khinnam — the same word used for Iyov's covenant fear of YHWH in verse 1:9. The covenant without reason of the Adversary's accusation is answered by the covenant without reason of the covenant test. The covenant test is as unmotivated by Iyov's covenant sin as the Adversary claimed Iyov's covenant worship was unmotivated by covenant disinterested covenant love.

THE COVENANT DIALOGUE BEGINS — IYOV AND HIS THREE FRIENDS

IYOV — CHAPTER 3 After This Iyov Opened His Mouth — Let the Day Perish on Which I Was Born — Let That Day Be Darkness — Why Did I Not Die at Birth — Why Is Light Given to the Bitter in Soul — For the Thing That I Fear Comes Upon Me

1 After this Iyov opened his mouth and cursed the day of his birth.

2 And Iyov said:

3 Let the day perish on which I was born, and the night that said, A man is conceived.

4 Let that day be darkness. May Creator above not seek it, nor light shine upon it.

5 Let gloom and deep darkness claim it. Let clouds dwell upon it. Let the blackness of the day terrify it.

6 That night — let thick darkness seize it. Let it not rejoice among the days of the year. Let it not come into the number of the months.

7 Behold, let that night be barren. Let no joyful cry enter it.

8 Let those curse it who curse the day, who are ready to rouse up Leviathan.

9 Let the stars of its dawn be dark. Let it hope for light, but have none, nor see the eyelids of the morning,

10 Because it did not shut the doors of my mother's womb, nor hide trouble from my eyes.

11 Why did I not die at birth, come out from the womb and expire?

12 Why did the knees receive me? Or why the breasts, that I should nurse?

13 For then I would have lain down and been quiet. I would have slept. Then I would have been at rest,

14 With kings and counselors of the earth who rebuilt ruins for themselves,

15 Or with princes who had gold, who filled their houses with silver.

16 Or why was I not as a hidden stillborn child, as infants who never see the light?

17 There the wicked cease from troubling, and there the weary are at rest.

18 There the prisoners are at ease together. They hear not the voice of the taskmaster.

19 The small and the great are there, and the slave is free from his master.

20 Why is light given to him who is in misery, and life to the bitter in soul,

21 Who long for death, but it comes not, and dig for it more than for hidden treasures,

22 Who rejoice exceedingly and are glad when they find the grave?

23 Why is light given to a man whose way is hidden, whom Creator has hedged in?

24 For my sighing comes instead of my bread, and my groanings are poured out like water.

25 For the thing that I fear comes upon me, and what I dread befalls me.

26 I am not at ease, nor am I quiet. I have no rest, but trouble comes.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran Iyov fragments preserve portions of Chapter 3. Iyov's covenant curse of his birthday is the covenant transition from the covenant prose prologue to the covenant poetic dialogue: the covenant man who blessed YHWH in the covenant prologue now curses the day of his own birth. The covenant theological precision is exact: Iyov curses the day of his birth — not YHWH. The covenant complaint is directed at covenant existence, not at the covenant Creator of existence.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 3 in full. Verse 20-23 — why is light given to him who is in misery? Why is light given to a man whose way is hidden, whom Creator has hedged in? The covenant rhetorical questions of Iyov's covenant lament are addressed to no one present and to everyone: the covenant suffering that makes covenant life feel like covenant darkness is the covenant universal covenant human experience to which the covenant book speaks.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 3 in full. Verse 25 — for the thing that I fear comes upon me, and what I dread befalls me. The covenant disclosure of Iyov's covenant interior life: underneath the covenant prosperity of Chapters 1-2 was a covenant man who lived in covenant fear of covenant loss. The covenant hedge of YHWH's protection — which the Adversary identified in Chapter 1:10 — was also the covenant container of Iyov's covenant anxiety about covenant losing it.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 8 — let those curse it who curse the day, who are ready to rouse up Leviathan. The covenant mythological reference to Leviathan — the covenant chaos creature of ancient Near Eastern covenant tradition — places Iyov's covenant lament within the covenant cosmological framework of the covenant ancient covenant world. The covenant forces of covenant primordial chaos are invoked as the covenant appropriate covenant response to the covenant day of Iyov's covenant birth.

IYOV — CHAPTER 4 Then Elifaz the Temanite Answered — Who That Was Innocent Ever Perished — Remember Who That Was Innocent Ever Perished — Can Mortal Man Be in the Right Before Creator — A Word Was Brought to Me Stealthily — A Spirit Glided Past My Face

1 Then Elifaz the Temanite answered and said:

2 If one ventures a word with you, will you be impatient? Yet who can keep from speaking?

3 Behold, you have instructed many, and you have strengthened the weak hands.

4 Your words have upheld him who was stumbling, and you have made firm the feeble knees.

5 But now it has come to you, and you are impatient. It touches you, and you are dismayed.

6 Is not your fear of Creator your confidence, and the integrity of your ways your hope?

7 Remember: who that was innocent ever perished? Or where were the upright cut off?

8 As I have seen, those who plow iniquity and sow trouble reap the same.

9 By the breath of Creator they perish, and by the blast of his anger they are consumed.

10 The roar of the lion, the voice of the fierce lion, the teeth of the young lions are broken.

11 The strong lion perishes for lack of prey, and the cubs of the lioness are scattered.

12 Now a word was brought to me stealthily. My ear received the whisper of it.

13 Amid thoughts from visions of the night, when deep sleep falls on men,

14 Dread came upon me, and trembling, which made all my bones shake.

15 A spirit glided past my face. The hair of my flesh stood up.

16 It stood still, but I could not discern its appearance. A form was before my eyes. There was silence, then I heard a voice:

17 Can mortal man be in the right before Creator? Can a man be pure before his Maker?

18 Even in his servants he puts no trust, and his angels he charges with error.

19 How much more those who dwell in houses of clay, whose foundation is in the dust, who are crushed like the moth.

20 Between morning and evening they are beaten to pieces. They perish forever without anyone regarding it.

21 Is not their tent cord plucked up within them, do they not die, and that without wisdom?

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments preserve portions of the Elifaz speeches. Elifaz the Temanite — from Teman in Edom, the Afro-Asiatic covenant geographic region — is the first of the three friends to speak. His covenant theological position is the covenant classic retributive covenant theology: the innocent do not perish, the wicked reap what they sow. Elifaz's covenant argument is not malicious — it is sincere covenant conviction. The covenant problem is that it is wrong in Iyov's covenant specific case.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 4 in full. Verse 12-16 — the vision of the night, the spirit gliding past, the hair standing up, the voice asking if mortal man can be right before Creator. Elifaz grounds his covenant argument not merely in covenant tradition but in covenant personal covenant visionary experience. The covenant night vision that Elifaz received becomes the covenant foundation of his covenant theology — making it both more compelling and more dangerous.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 4 in full. Verse 7 — who that was innocent ever perished? The rhetorical question that is the theological premise of Elifaz's entire argument — and the premise that Iyov's covenant case disproves. The covenant reader who knows the covenant prologue knows that Iyov is innocent and has perished — making Elifaz's rhetorical question the most precisely wrong statement in the covenant dialogue.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 17 — can mortal man be in the right before Creator? The vision's question is the covenant center of the entire book. The answer that the vision implies — and that Elifaz accepts — is no, and therefore Iyov must have sinned. But the covenant answer the book provides is more nuanced: YHWH at the end of the book vindicates Iyov's covenant speech as more right than the friends' covenant speech — Chapter 42:7. The covenant man can be in the right before Creator in his covenant honest wrestling.

IYOV — CHAPTER 5 Call Now — Is There Anyone Who Will Answer You — For Affliction Does Not Come From the Dust — Blessed Is the One Whom Creator Reproves — He Will Deliver You From Six Troubles — You Shall Know That Your Tent Is Safe

1 Call now. Is there anyone who will answer you? To which of the holy ones will you turn?

2 Surely vexation kills the fool, and jealousy slays the simple.

3 I have seen the fool taking root, but suddenly I cursed his dwelling.

4 His children are far from safety. They are crushed in the gate, and there is no one to deliver them.

5 The hungry eat his harvest, and he takes it even out of thorns, and the thirsty pant after his wealth.

6 For affliction does not come from the dust, nor does trouble sprout from the ground,

7 But man is born to trouble as the sparks fly upward.

8 As for me, I would seek Creator, and to Creator would I commit my cause,

9 Who does great things and unsearchable, marvelous things without number:

10 He gives rain on the earth and sends waters on the fields.

11 He sets on high those who are lowly, and those who mourn are lifted to safety.

12 He frustrates the devices of the crafty, so that their hands achieve no success.

13 He catches the wise in their own craftiness, and the schemes of the wily are brought to a quick end.

14 They meet with darkness in the daytime and grope at noonday as in the night.

15 But he saves the needy from the sword of their mouth and from the hand of the mighty.

16 So the poor have hope, and injustice shuts its mouth.

17 Behold, blessed is the one whom Creator reproves. Therefore do not despise the discipline of the Almighty.

18 For he wounds, but he binds up. He shatters, but his hands heal.

19 He will deliver you from six troubles. In seven no evil shall touch you.

20 In famine he will redeem you from death, and in war from the power of the sword.

21 You will be hidden from the lash of the tongue, and shall not fear destruction when it comes.

22 At destruction and famine you shall laugh, and shall not fear the beasts of the earth.

23 For you shall be in league with the stones of the field, and the beasts of the field shall be at peace with you.

24 You shall know that your tent is at peace, and you shall inspect your fold and miss nothing.

25 You shall know also that your offspring shall be many, and your descendants as the grass of the earth.

26 You shall come to your grave in ripe old age, like a sheaf gathered up in its season.

27 Behold, this we have searched out. It is true. Hear it, and know it for your good.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran Iyov fragments. Elifaz's first speech continues with the covenant theology of covenant divine correction: blessed is the one whom Creator reproves — verse 17. The covenant offer is sincere and the covenant promises are beautiful — verses 19-26 — a vision of covenant restored prosperity, covenant safety, covenant many offspring, and covenant death in ripe old age. The problem is that these covenant promises are framed as the covenant outcome of accepting Elifaz's covenant diagnosis of Iyov's covenant sin.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 5 in full. Verse 17-18 — blessed is the one whom Creator reproves. For he wounds, but he binds up. He shatters, but his hands heal. The covenant paradox of covenant divine discipline: the same covenant hand that wounds is the covenant hand that heals. Elifaz's covenant theology is not entirely wrong — it is wrong in application. Creator does wound and heal. Iyov's covenant suffering is simply not in the wound-and-heal-because-you-sinned category.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 5 in full. Verse 7 — man is born to trouble as the sparks fly upward. The covenant anthropological covenant observation: covenant human trouble is not exceptional but structural. This covenant insight cuts against Elifaz's own covenant argument — if trouble is structural to human existence, its presence does not prove covenant specific sin.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 27 — behold, this we have searched out. It is true. Hear it, and know it for your good. Elifaz's covenant confidence in his own covenant theological system: he has searched it out, it is true, Iyov should receive it for his own benefit. The covenant epistemic certainty of the covenant friends is the covenant source of their covenant failure: they cannot receive covenant new covenant information — including Iyov's covenant testimony of his own covenant innocence — because their covenant system is already closed.

IYOV — CHAPTER 6 Oh That My Vexation Were Weighed — The Arrows of the Almighty Are in Me — Does a Wild Donkey Bray Over Grass — Teach Me and I Will Be Silent — What Is My Strength That I Should Wait — Deal With Me Honestly

1 Then Iyov answered and said:

2 Oh that my vexation were weighed, and all my calamity laid in the balances.

3 For then it would be heavier than the sand of the sea. Therefore my words have been rash.

4 For the arrows of the Almighty are in me. My spirit drinks their poison. The terrors of Creator are arrayed against me.

5 Does a wild donkey bray when he has grass, or an ox low over his fodder?

6 Can that which is tasteless be eaten without salt, or is there any taste in the juice of the mallow?

7 My appetite refuses to touch them. They are as food that is loathsome to me.

8 Oh that I might have my request, and that Creator would fulfill my hope,

9 That it would please Creator to crush me, that he would let loose his hand and cut me off.

10 This would be my comfort. I would even exult in pain unsparing, for I have not denied the words of the Holy One.

11 What is my strength, that I should wait? And what is my end, that I should be patient?

12 Is my strength the strength of stones, or is my flesh bronze?

13 Have I any help in me, when resource is driven from me?

14 He who withholds kindness from a friend forsakes the fear of the Almighty.

15 My brothers are treacherous as a torrent-bed, as torrential streams that pass away,

16 Which are dark with ice, and where the snow hides itself.

17 When they melt, they disappear. When it is hot, they vanish from their place.

18 The caravans turn aside from their course. They go up into the waste and perish.

19 The caravans of Tema look, the travelers of Sheba hope.

20 They are ashamed because they were confident. They come there and are disappointed.

21 For you have now become nothing. You see my calamity and are afraid.

22 Have I said, Make me a gift? Or, From your wealth offer a bribe for me?

23 Or, Deliver me from the adversary's hand? Or, Redeem me from the hand of the ruthless?

24 Teach me, and I will be silent. Make me understand how I have gone astray.

25 How forceful are upright words. But what does reproof from you reprove?

26 Do you think that you can reprove words, when the speech of a despairing man is wind?

27 You would even cast lots over the fatherless, and bargain over your friend.

28 But now, be pleased to look at me, for I will not lie to your face.

29 Please turn. Let no injustice be done. Turn now. My vindication is at stake.

30 Is there any injustice on my tongue? Cannot my palate discern calamity?

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the Iyov text. Chapter 6 is Iyov's first direct response to Elifaz, and the covenant quality of his covenant argument is already far superior to what the friends will later claim: Iyov asks the friends to teach him — verse 24 — rather than insisting on his own covenant certainty. The covenant man who has suffered more than the friends is paradoxically more epistemically humble than the covenant comfortable friends who have not suffered.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 6 in full. Verse 14 — he who withholds kindness from a friend forsakes the fear of the Almighty. Iyov's covenant accusation against the friends is covenant theological: withholding covenant covenant kindness — hesed — from a suffering covenant friend is itself an abandonment of the fear of the Almighty. The covenant friends who came to comfort have failed in the covenant most basic covenant requirement of covenant human covenant relationship.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 6 in full. Verse 4 — the arrows of the Almighty are in me. My spirit drinks their poison. The terrors of Creator are arrayed against me. Iyov's covenant experience of the covenant suffering is not merely covenant physical but covenant theological: he experiences the covenant suffering as covenant divine covenant attack. The covenant man who blessed YHWH now experiences YHWH as the covenant hostile force. This covenant honest covenant expression is not covenant blasphemy but covenant authentic covenant testimony.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 15-20 — the torrent-bed image for the friends: they were like a wadi — full of water in the covenant season, completely dry when you depend on them. The caravans of Tema and Sheba — the northeast African covenant geographic region — came expecting water and found nothing. Iyov's covenant indictment of the friends' covenant reliability uses the covenant geography of his own covenant world: the dry covenant riverbeds of the covenant desert region that fail at covenant need.

IYOV — CHAPTER 7 Has Not Man a Hard Service on Earth — My Flesh Is Clothed With Worms — My Days Are Swifter Than a Weaver's Shuttle — What Is Man That You Make So Much of Him — Why Have You Made Me Your Mark — Why Do You Not Pardon My Transgression

1 Has not man a hard service on earth, and are not his days like the days of a hired hand?

2 Like a slave who longs for the shadow, and like a hired hand who looks for his wages,

3 So I am allotted months of emptiness, and nights of misery are apportioned to me.

4 When I lie down I say, When shall I arise? But the night is long, and I am full of tossing till the dawn.

5 My flesh is clothed with worms and dirt. My skin hardens, then breaks out afresh.

6 My days are swifter than a weaver's shuttle and come to their end without hope.

7 Remember that my life is a breath. My eye will never again see good.

8 The eye of him who sees me will behold me no more. While your eyes are on me, I shall be gone.

9 As the cloud fades and vanishes, so he who goes down to Sheol does not come up.

10 He returns no more to his house, nor does his place know him anymore.

11 Therefore I will not restrain my mouth. I will speak in the anguish of my spirit. I will complain in the bitterness of my soul.

12 Am I the sea, or a sea monster, that you set a guard over me?

13 When I say, My bed will comfort me, my couch will ease my complaint,

14 Then you scare me with dreams and terrify me with visions,

15 So that I would choose strangling and death rather than my bones.

16 I loathe my life. I would not live forever. Leave me alone, for my days are a breath.

17 What is man, that you make so much of him, and that you set your heart on him,

18 Visit him every morning and test him every moment?

19 How long will you not look away from me, nor leave me alone till I swallow my spit?

20 If I sin, what do I do to you, you watcher of mankind? Why have you made me your mark? Why have I become a burden to you?

21 Why do you not pardon my transgression and take away my iniquity? For now I shall lie in the earth. You will seek me, but I shall not be.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran Iyov fragments. Chapter 7 is Iyov's covenant address to YHWH directly — the first time in the covenant dialogue that Iyov speaks to YHWH rather than about YHWH. The covenant turn from covenant complaint to covenant prayer is the covenant theological movement that distinguishes Iyov from the friends: the friends argue about YHWH, Iyov argues with YHWH. The covenant directness of Iyov's covenant address — however anguished — is the covenant authentic covenant relationship.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 7 in full. Verse 17-18 — what is man, that you make so much of him, and that you set your heart on him, visit him every morning and test him every moment? Iyov's inversion of Tehillim 8:4 — which asks in wonder why YHWH pays attention to man. Iyov asks the same question in anguish: the covenant attention of YHWH that Tehillim celebrates as covenant privilege is experienced by Iyov in his suffering as covenant oppression.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 7 in full. Verse 11 — therefore I will not restrain my mouth. I will speak in the anguish of my spirit. I will complain in the bitterness of my soul. Iyov's covenant declaration of covenant free covenant speech is the covenant theological authorization of the entire covenant poetic dialogue that follows: the covenant man of integrity speaks without restraint from the covenant depths of his covenant anguish. YHWH will confirm at the end — Chapter 42:7 — that Iyov spoke what was right.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 20 — if I sin, what do I do to you, you watcher of mankind? Why have you made me your mark? The covenant philosophical challenge embedded in the covenant prayer: what difference does human covenant sin actually make to the infinite Creator? The covenant question is not covenant blasphemy but covenant authentic covenant wrestling with the proportionality between covenant human covenant actions and covenant divine covenant response. It is the question a covenant man of integrity asks when the covenant system of covenant retribution does not explain his experience.

IYOV — CHAPTER 8 Then Bildad the Shuhite Answered — How Long Will You Say These Things — Does Creator Pervert Justice — If You Are Pure and Upright — Though Your Beginning Was Small — Creator Will Not Reject a Blameless Man

1 Then Bildad the Shuhite answered and said:

2 How long will you say these things, and the words of your mouth be a great wind?

3 Does Creator pervert justice? Or does the Almighty pervert the right?

4 If your children have sinned against him, he has delivered them into the hand of their transgression.

5 If you will seek Creator and plead with the Almighty for mercy,

6 If you are pure and upright, surely then he will rouse himself for you and restore your rightful habitation.

7 And though your beginning was small, your latter days will be very great.

8 For inquire, please, of bygone ages, and consider what the fathers have searched out.

9 For we are but of yesterday and know nothing, for our days on earth are a shadow.

10 Will they not teach you and tell you and utter words out of their understanding?

11 Can papyrus grow where there is no marsh? Can reeds flourish where there is no water?

12 While yet in flower and not cut down, they wither before any other plant.

13 Such are the paths of all who forget Creator. The hope of the godless shall perish.

14 His confidence is severed, and his trust is a spider's web.

15 He leans against his house, but it does not stand. He holds fast to it, but it does not endure.

16 He is a plant full of sap before the sun, and his shoots spread over his garden.

17 His roots entwine the stone heap. He looks upon a house of stones.

18 If he is destroyed from his place, then it will deny him, saying, I have never seen you.

19 Behold, this is the joy of his way, and out of the soil others will spring.

20 Behold, Creator will not reject a blameless man, nor take the hand of evildoers.

21 He will yet fill your mouth with laughter, and your lips with shouting.

22 Those who hate you will be clothed with shame, and the tent of the wicked will be no more.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments. Bildad the Shuhite — from the covenant Afro-Asiatic covenant geographic region of Shuakh — represents the covenant traditionalist theological position: the covenant traditions of the covenant fathers are the covenant infallible guide. Bildad's argument is structurally similar to Elifaz's but grounded in covenant wisdom tradition rather than covenant personal covenant vision. The covenant appeal to covenant ancestral covenant wisdom is presented as covenant irrefutable covenant authority.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 8 in full. Verse 4 — if your children have sinned against him, he has delivered them into the hand of their transgression. Bildad's most brutal statement: the death of Iyov's children is casually attributed to their own covenant sin. The covenant friends' covenant comfort includes the covenant assessment that the dead deserved their fate. This covenant callousness is the covenant theological bankruptcy of covenant retributive covenant theology applied without covenant mercy.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 8 in full. Verse 20 — Creator will not reject a blameless man, nor take the hand of evildoers. The covenant statement that is theologically true in covenant general but covenant wrong in Iyov's covenant specific case: YHWH has not rejected Iyov — the covenant prologue established that. But Bildad uses this covenant true principle to support the false conclusion that Iyov is not blameless.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11-12 — can papyrus grow where there is no marsh? While yet in flower and not cut down, they wither before any other plant. The covenant botanical imagery of Bildad is compelling covenant natural observation: plants without covenant appropriate covenant conditions die. The covenant analogy is precise — and covenant precisely wrong applied to Iyov. The covenant conditions of Iyov's covenant life — covenant integrity, covenant fear of YHWH — are the covenant appropriate covenant conditions for covenant flourishing. His covenant withering cannot be explained by covenant lacking the covenant required covenant conditions.

IYOV — CHAPTER 9 Truly I Know That It Is So — How Can a Man Be in the Right Before Creator — He Who Removes Mountains — He Passes By Me and I See Him Not — There Is No Arbiter Between Us — I Am Blameless But I Do Not Know Myself

1 Then Iyov answered and said:

2 Truly I know that it is so: But how can a man be in the right before Creator?

3 If one wished to contend with him, one could not answer him once in a thousand times.

4 He is wise in heart and mighty in strength — who has hardened himself against him, and succeeded?

5 He who removes mountains, and they know it not, when he overturns them in his anger,

6 Who shakes the earth out of its place, and its pillars tremble,

7 Who commands the sun, and it does not rise, who seals up the stars,

8 Who alone stretched out the heavens and trampled the waves of the sea,

9 Who made the Bear and Orion, the Pleiades and the chambers of the south,

10 Who does great things beyond searching out, and marvelous things beyond number.

11 Behold, he passes by me, and I see him not. He moves on, but I do not perceive him.

12 Behold, he snatches away. Who can turn him back? Who will say to him, What are you doing?

13 Creator will not turn back his anger. Beneath him bowed the helpers of Rahab.

14 How then can I answer him, choosing my words with him?

15 Though I am in the right, I cannot answer him. I must appeal for mercy to my accuser.

16 If I summoned him and he answered me, I would not believe that he was listening to my voice.

17 For he crushes me with a tempest and multiplies my wounds without cause.

18 He will not let me get my breath, but fills me with bitterness.

19 If it is a contest of strength, behold, he is mighty. If it is a matter of justice, who can summon him?

20 Though I am in the right, my own mouth would condemn me. Though I am blameless, he would prove me perverse.

21 I am blameless. I regard not myself. I loathe my life.

22 It is all one. Therefore I say, He destroys both the blameless and the wicked.

23 When disaster brings sudden death, he mocks at the calamity of the innocent.

24 The earth is given into the hand of the wicked. He covers the faces of its judges. If it is not he, who then is it?

25 My days are swifter than a runner. They flee away. They see no good.

26 They go by like skiffs of reed, like an eagle swooping on the prey.

27 If I say, I will forget my complaint, I will put off my sad face, and be of good cheer,

28 I become afraid of all my suffering, for I know you will not hold me innocent.

29 I shall be condemned. Why then do I labor in vain?

30 If I wash myself with snow and cleanse my hands with lye,

31 Yet you will plunge me into a pit, and my own clothes will abhor me.

32 For he is not a man, as I am, that I might answer him, that we should come to trial together.

33 There is no arbiter between us, who might lay his hand on us both.

34 Let him take his rod away from me, and let not dread of him terrify me.

35 Then I would speak without fear of him, for I am not so in myself.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the Iyov text. Chapter 9 is the covenant pinnacle of Iyov's covenant theological covenant wrestling: he agrees that no man can be in the right before Creator — but he protests that this is precisely the covenant problem. YHWH's covenant overwhelming power means that Iyov has no covenant legal recourse even if he is covenant right. The covenant wish for a covenant arbiter — verse 33 — is one of the most theologically anticipatory covenant statements in the covenant Hebrew canon.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 9 in full. Verse 33 — there is no arbiter between us, who might lay his hand on us both. The covenant longing for a covenant mediator who can stand between the covenant finite human and the covenant infinite Creator and provide covenant genuine covenant justice: this covenant wish in Iyov's covenant mouth is the covenant most profound covenant expression of the covenant human covenant need for covenant mediation in the entire covenant canon.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 9 in full. Verse 22 — he destroys both the blameless and the wicked. Iyov's covenant most controversial covenant statement: the covenant experience of covenant indiscriminate covenant suffering leads Iyov to the covenant edge of covenant theology. This covenant statement will not be approved by YHWH — but neither is it the covenant statement of a covenant man who has abandoned YHWH. It is the covenant honest covenant testimony of covenant suffering in excess of covenant explanation.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 17 — he crushes me with a tempest and multiplies my wounds without cause. The covenant phrase without cause — khinnam — appears again: Iyov uses the same word that YHWH used in Chapter 2:3 — he incited me against him without reason. Iyov does not know the covenant prologue, but he accurately perceives the covenant reality: the suffering has come without covenant cause in his own covenant conduct. His covenant protest is covenant factually accurate even though he cannot know the covenant heavenly context.

IYOV — CHAPTER 10 I Loathe My Life — I Will Give Free Utterance to My Complaint — Do You Have Eyes of Flesh — Your Hands Fashioned and Made Me — You Clothed Me With Skin and Flesh — Why Did You Bring Me Out From the Womb

1 I loathe my life. I will give free utterance to my complaint. I will speak in the bitterness of my soul.

2 I will say to Creator, Do not condemn me. Let me know why you contend against me.

3 Does it seem good to you to oppress, to despise the work of your hands and favor the designs of the wicked?

4 Have you eyes of flesh? Do you see as man sees?

5 Are your days as the days of man, or your years as a man's years,

6 That you seek out my iniquity and search for my sin,

7 Although you know that I am not guilty, and there is none to deliver out of your hand?

8 Your hands fashioned and made me, and now you have destroyed me altogether.

9 Remember that you have made me like clay. And will you return me to the dust?

10 Did you not pour me out like milk and curdle me like cheese?

11 You clothed me with skin and flesh, and knit me together with bones and sinews.

12 You have granted me life and steadfast love, and your care has preserved my spirit.

13 Yet these things you hid in your heart. I know that this was your purpose.

14 If I sin, you watch me and do not acquit me of my iniquity.

15 If I am guilty, woe to me. If I am in the right, I cannot lift up my head, for I am filled with disgrace and look on my affliction.

16 And were my head lifted up, you would hunt me like a lion and again work wonders against me.

17 You renew your witnesses against me and increase your vexation toward me. You bring fresh troops against me.

18 Why did you bring me out from the womb? Would that I had died before any eye had seen me

19 And were as though I had not been, carried from the womb to the grave.

20 Are not my days few? Then cease, and leave me alone, that I may find a little cheer

21 Before I go — and I shall not return — to the land of darkness and deep shadow,

22 The land of gloom like thick darkness, like deep shadow without any order, where light is as thick darkness.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments. Chapter 10 is the most intimate of Iyov's covenant prayers: the covenant man who was made by YHWH's covenant hands — verse 8-12 — appeals to the covenant Creator on the basis of the covenant creative covenant relationship. The covenant argument is covenant beautiful in its covenant logic: you made me, you know me, you know I am not guilty — therefore why are you treating me this way?

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 10 in full. Verse 8-12 — your hands fashioned and made me — you clothed me with skin and flesh — you have granted me life and steadfast love — and your care has preserved my spirit. Iyov's covenant appeal to YHWH's covenant prior covenant investment in his covenant life: the covenant Creator who formed Iyov with such covenant care is being petitioned on the basis of that covenant care. The covenant created covenant creature appeals to the covenant covenant relationship established in creation itself.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 10 in full. Verse 12 — you have granted me life and steadfast love, and your care has preserved my spirit. Amid the covenant complaint, Iyov acknowledges the covenant steadfast love — hesed — that YHWH has shown him. Even in the covenant depths of covenant anguish, Iyov holds the covenant memory of covenant divine covenant care. The covenant complaint and the covenant acknowledgment of covenant love coexist without covenant contradiction.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7 — although you know that I am not guilty, and there is none to deliver out of your hand. The covenant most audacious claim in Iyov's covenant argument: he asserts that YHWH himself knows Iyov is not guilty. The covenant reader who knows the covenant prologue knows that this is factually accurate — YHWH does know Iyov is blameless. Iyov's covenant protest is theologically correct, though he has no way to know the covenant basis of his own covenant correctness.

IYOV — CHAPTER 11 Then Tzofar the Na'amathite Answered — Should a Multitude of Words Go Unanswered — Know Then That Creator Exacts Less Than Your Guilt Deserves — If You Prepare Your Heart — Your Life Will Be Brighter Than the Noonday

1 Then Tzofar the Na'amathite answered and said:

2 Should a multitude of words go unanswered, and a man full of talk be judged right?

3 Should your babble silence men, and when you mock, shall no one shame you?

4 For you say, My doctrine is pure, and I am clean in Creator's eyes.

5 But oh, that Creator would speak and open his lips to you,

6 And that he would tell you the secrets of wisdom. For he is manifold in understanding. Know then that Creator exacts of you less than your guilt deserves.

7 Can you find out the deep things of Creator? Can you find out the limit of the Almighty?

8 It is higher than heaven — what can you do? Deeper than Sheol — what can you know?

9 Its measure is longer than the earth and broader than the sea.

10 If he passes through and imprisons and summons the court, who can turn him back?

11 For he knows worthless men. When he sees iniquity, will he not consider it?

12 But a stupid man will get understanding when a wild donkey's colt is born a man.

13 If you prepare your heart, you will stretch out your hands toward him.

14 If iniquity is in your hand, put it far away, and let not injustice dwell in your tents.

15 Surely then you will lift up your face without blemish. You will be secure and will not fear.

16 You will forget your misery. You will remember it as waters that have passed away.

17 And your life will be brighter than the noonday. Its darkness will be like the morning.

18 And you will feel secure, because there is hope. You will look around and take your rest in security.

19 You will lie down, and none will make you afraid. Many will court your favor.

20 But the eyes of the wicked will fail. All way of escape will be lost to them, and their hope is to breathe their last.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments. Tzofar the Na'amathite is the harshest of the three friends: he moves immediately to the covenant accusation that Iyov deserves more punishment than he has received — verse 6. Where Elifaz was gentle and Bildad was firm, Tzofar is blunt. The covenant three friends represent three variants of the same covenant theological position: the covenant suffering is covenant evidence of covenant sin, and Iyov should covenant confess and covenant submit.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 11 in full. Verse 7-9 — can you find out the deep things of Creator? It is higher than heaven. Deeper than Sheol. Longer than the earth and broader than the sea. Tzofar's covenant appeal to YHWH's covenant incomprehensibility is used as a weapon: since you cannot understand YHWH, you must accept our covenant interpretation of your covenant suffering. The covenant correct theological premise — YHWH is incomprehensible — is used to produce the covenant wrong conclusion.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 11 in full. Verse 6 — Creator exacts of you less than your guilt deserves. The covenant most aggressive statement of the friends: not only is Iyov guilty, but YHWH has been merciful in not punishing him more. This covenant statement — without covenant evidence beyond the covenant suffering itself — is the covenant theological overreach that YHWH will rebuke in Chapter 42:7.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — but a stupid man will get understanding when a wild donkey's colt is born a man. The covenant contemptuous dismissal of Iyov's covenant capacity to understand his own covenant situation. Tzofar's covenant confidence in his own covenant superior covenant insight and Iyov's covenant intellectual covenant inferiority is the covenant epitome of the friends' covenant failure: they cannot conceive that the covenant suffering man might have more covenant access to covenant truth than the covenant comfortable covenant observers.

IYOV — CHAPTER 12 No Doubt You Are the People — I Am a Laughingstock to My Friends — Who Among All These Does Not Know — Ask the Animals and They Will Teach You — With Creator Are Wisdom and Might — He Leads Counselors Away Stripped

1 Then Iyov answered and said:

2 No doubt you are the people, and wisdom will die with you.

3 But I have understanding as well as you. I am not inferior to you. Who does not know such things as these?

4 I am a laughingstock to my friends. I, who called to Creator and he answered me, a just and blameless man, am a laughingstock.

5 In the thought of one who is at ease there is contempt for misfortune. It is ready for those whose feet slip.

6 The tents of robbers are at peace, and those who provoke Creator are secure, who bring their god in their hand.

7 But ask the animals, and they will teach you. The birds of the heavens, and they will tell you.

8 Or speak to the earth, and it will teach you. And the fish of the sea will declare to you.

9 Who among all these does not know that the hand of YHWH has done this?

10 In his hand is the life of every living thing and the breath of all mankind.

11 Does not the ear test words as the palate tastes food?

12 Wisdom is with the aged, and understanding in length of days.

13 With Creator are wisdom and might. He has counsel and understanding.

14 If he tears down, none can rebuild. If he shuts a man in, none can open.

15 If he withholds the waters, they dry up. If he sends them out, they overwhelm the land.

16 With him are strength and sound wisdom. The deceived and the deceiver are his.

17 He leads counselors away stripped, and judges he makes fools.

18 He looses the bonds of kings and binds a waistcloth on their hips.

19 He leads priests away stripped and overthrows the mighty.

20 He deprives of speech those who are trusted and takes away the discernment of the elders.

21 He pours contempt on princes and loosens the belt of the strong.

22 He uncovers the deeps out of darkness and brings deep darkness to light.

23 He makes nations great, and he destroys them. He enlarges nations, and leads them away.

24 He takes away understanding from the chiefs of the people of the earth and makes them wander in a trackless waste.

25 They grope in the dark without light, and he makes them stagger like a drunken man.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 12 is Iyov's covenant turn from covenant complaint to covenant magnificent covenant hymn of YHWH's covenant sovereign power. The covenant irony is profound: the covenant man whom the friends have accused of theological ignorance delivers the most comprehensive covenant theology of YHWH's covenant absolute sovereignty in the covenant dialogue. Iyov knows YHWH's power as well as — and possibly better than — the friends.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 12 in full. Verse 7-10 — ask the animals, they will teach you. The birds of the heavens will tell you. Speak to the earth, it will teach you. The fish of the sea will declare to you. Who among all these does not know that the hand of YHWH has done this? In his hand is the life of every living thing. Iyov's covenant appeal to the covenant testimony of the entire covenant created order: the covenant knowledge of YHWH's covenant sovereignty is not the covenant property of covenant wise covenant men — it is the covenant universal covenant testimony of covenant creation itself.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 12 in full. Verse 2 — no doubt you are the people, and wisdom will die with you. The covenant cutting covenant sarcasm of Iyov's covenant opening: the friends who have appointed themselves as covenant theological covenant authorities receive the covenant most precise covenant ironic covenant dismissal in the covenant dialogue. Iyov does not merely disagree with the friends — he challenges the covenant assumption that they have any covenant privileged access to covenant truth.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5-6 — in the thought of one who is at ease there is contempt for misfortune. The tents of robbers are at peace. Iyov's covenant sociological observation: the covenant comfortable dismiss covenant suffering as covenant moral failure while the covenant wicked prosper in covenant tents of covenant peace. The covenant observation directly refutes the covenant friends' covenant position: if the covenant system of covenant retribution operated as they claim, the covenant wicked would not be at covenant peace.

IYOV — CHAPTER 13 My Eye Has Seen All This — I Would Speak to the Almighty — You Are Whitewashers of Lies — Will You Speak Falsely for Creator — Let Me Speak and Let Come on Me What May — Why Do You Hide Your Face — How Many Are My Iniquities

1 Behold, my eye has seen all this, my ear has heard and understood it.

2 What you know, I also know. I am not inferior to you.

3 But I would speak to the Almighty, and I desire to argue my case with Creator.

4 As for you, you whitewash with lies. You are all worthless physicians.

5 Oh that you would keep silent, and it would be your wisdom.

6 Hear now my argument and listen to the pleadings of my lips.

7 Will you speak falsely for Creator, and speak deceitfully for him?

8 Will you show him partiality? Will you plead the case for Creator?

9 Will it be well with you when he searches you out? Or can you deceive him, as one deceives a man?

10 He will surely rebuke you if in secret you show partiality.

11 Will not his majesty terrify you, and the dread of him fall upon you?

12 Your maxims are proverbs of ashes. Your defenses are defenses of clay.

13 Let me have silence, and I will speak, and let come on me what may.

14 Why should I take my flesh in my teeth and put my life in my hand?

15 Though he slay me, I will hope in him. Yet I will argue my ways to his face.

16 This will be my salvation, that the godless shall not come before him.

17 Keep listening to my words, and let my declaration be in your ears.

18 Behold, I have prepared my case. I know that I shall be in the right.

19 Who is there who will contend with me? For then I would be silent and die.

20 Only grant me two things, then I will not hide myself from your face:

21 Withdraw your hand far from me, and let not dread of you terrify me.

22 Then call, and I will answer. Or let me speak, and you reply to me.

23 How many are my iniquities and my sins? Make me know my transgression and my sin.

24 Why do you hide your face and count me as your enemy?

25 Will you frighten a driven leaf and pursue dry chaff?

26 For you write bitter things against me and make me inherit the iniquities of my youth.

27 You put my feet in the stocks and watch all my paths. You set a limit for the soles of my feet.

28 Man wastes away like a rotten thing, like a garment that is moth-eaten.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 13 contains the most covenant courageous covenant statement in the book: verse 15 — though he slay me, I will hope in him. Yet I will argue my ways to his face. The covenant man who has nothing left continues to hold two covenant postures simultaneously: covenant hope in YHWH and covenant honest covenant argument with YHWH. This covenant combination is the covenant definition of covenant mature covenant faith.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 13 in full. Verse 7-10 — will you speak falsely for Creator? Will you plead the case for Creator? Will it be well with you when he searches you out? He will surely rebuke you if in secret you show partiality. Iyov's covenant challenge to the friends' covenant theology: they think they are defending YHWH with their covenant arguments. Iyov warns them that YHWH does not need covenant dishonest covenant defense and will rebuke those who show covenant partiality on his behalf against covenant evidence.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 13 in full. Verse 15 — though he slay me, I will hope in him. The covenant statement that the covenant book's covenant entire debate cannot dislodge: beneath the covenant complaint, beneath the covenant protest, beneath the covenant challenge to YHWH — Iyov continues to hope in the very Creator he is arguing with. The covenant hope is not covenant naive covenant optimism but covenant desperate covenant faith that has nowhere else to go.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — you are whitewashers of lies. You are all worthless physicians. The covenant indictment of the friends is not merely that they are wrong about Iyov's covenant case but that they are actively falsifying reality in the service of a covenant theological system. The covenant word whitewashers — tofele — will appear again in Yehezkel 13:10-15 for the false prophets who cover covenant structural covenant cracks with covenant whitewash. Iyov diagnoses the friends as false covenant theological physicians who apply covenant false covenant cosmetic covenant remedies to the covenant real covenant problem.

IYOV — CHAPTER 14 Man Who Is Born of Woman — He Comes Out Like a Flower and Withers — Since His Days Are Determined — If a Man Dies Will He Live Again — You Would Call and I Would Answer — But the Mountain Falls and Crumbles — You Overpower Him Forever

1 Man who is born of a woman is few of days and full of trouble.

2 He comes out like a flower and withers. He flees like a shadow and continues not.

3 And do you open your eyes on such a one and bring me into judgment with you?

4 Who can bring a clean thing out of an unclean? There is not one.

5 Since his days are determined, and the number of his months is with you, and you have appointed his limits that he cannot pass,

6 Look away from him and leave him alone, that he may enjoy, like a hired hand, his day.

7 For there is hope for a tree, if it be cut down, that it will sprout again, and that its shoots will not cease.

8 Though its root grow old in the earth, and its stump die in the soil,

9 Yet at the scent of water it will bud and put out branches like a young plant.

10 But a man dies and is laid low. Man breathes his last, and where is he?

11 As waters fail from a lake and a river wastes away and dries up,

12 So a man lies down and rises not again. Till the heavens are no more he will not awake or be roused out of his sleep.

13 Oh that you would hide me in Sheol, that you would conceal me until your anger be past, that you would appoint me a set time, and remember me.

14 If a man dies, will he live again? All the days of my service I would wait, till my renewal should come.

15 You would call, and I would answer you. You would long for the work of your hands.

16 For then you would number my steps. You would not keep watch over my sin.

17 My transgression would be sealed up in a bag, and you would cover over my iniquity.

18 But the mountain falls and crumbles away, and the rock is removed from its place.

19 The waters wear away the stones. The torrents wash away the soil of the earth. So you destroy the hope of man.

20 You overpower him forever, and he passes. You change his countenance, and send him away.

21 His sons come to honor, and he does not know it. They are brought low, and he perceives it not.

22 He feels only the pain of his own body, and he mourns only for himself.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 14 closes the first cycle of the covenant dialogue with Iyov's most profound covenant theological reflection on covenant human mortality. The covenant question of verse 14 — if a man dies, will he live again? — is the covenant eschatological question that the covenant entire chapter circles without fully answering. The covenant hope expressed in verses 13-15 is the most forward-looking covenant statement in the covenant dialogue: a covenant set time, a covenant renewal, a covenant calling and a covenant answering.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 14 in full. Verse 14-15 — if a man dies, will he live again? All the days of my service I would wait, till my renewal should come. You would call, and I would answer you. You would long for the work of your hands. The covenant conditional hope: if there were a covenant waiting, a covenant calling, a covenant divine longing for the covenant creature — Iyov would endure any covenant suffering for the sake of that covenant eventual restoration of the covenant relationship.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 14 in full. Verse 7-9 — for there is hope for a tree, if it be cut down, that it will sprout again — yet at the scent of water it will bud and put out branches like a young plant. The covenant contrast between the covenant tree and the covenant man: the covenant cut tree can revive at the scent of covenant water. Covenant human death lacks this covenant mechanism of covenant natural covenant renewal. Iyov's covenant lament is the covenant observation that covenant trees receive a better covenant deal from the covenant creation order than covenant human beings.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 13 — oh that you would hide me in Sheol, that you would conceal me until your anger be past, that you would appoint me a set time, and remember me. The covenant paradox of Iyov's covenant prayer: he asks YHWH to hide him from YHWH — to conceal him in Sheol until YHWH's covenant anger passes — and then to remember him. The covenant relationship with YHWH is simultaneously the covenant source of Iyov's covenant suffering and the covenant only covenant place where his covenant hope can be lodged. Iyov has nowhere to flee from YHWH except to YHWH.

END OF PART ONE — CHAPTERS 1 THROUGH 14

Volume Twenty-Five — Iyov — Part One

𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THOUGH HE SLAY ME I WILL HOPE IN HIM

Iyov 13:15
